serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Dal Dum Pukht is approximately 300 g, typically served as a main dish alongside rice or Indian flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (cooked mixed lentil / dal preparations); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cream and ghee components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Dal Dum Pukht as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN published data for cooked mixed lentil preparations (dal) combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cream, butter, and ghee components, weighted to reflect the input per_100g values (energy 150 kcal, protein 6.0 g, fat 8.33 g, carbohydrate 11.67 g). The input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are cross-checked for plausibility against ICMR-NIN dal entries (typical range: energy 100–160 kcal/100g, protein 5–8 g, fat 4–10 g for enriched dal preparations) and found to be plausible; they are retained as the best available estimate. The original input listed 'https://foodo.sg/' as the HPB source URL — this URL does not correspond to the official HPB nutrient database and is not an approved source; it has been replaced with ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the primary approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Lentils, boiled (GI 29); used as analogous estimate for mixed cooked lentil preparation (Dal Dum Pukht)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dal Dum Pukht is a mixed lentil dish (urad dal, chana dal, toor dal) slow-cooked with cream and ghee. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of 29 for boiled lentils and GI values in the range 25–32 for various cooked lentil preparations. GI 29 is used as a conservative analogous estimate for this mixed lentil dish. The addition of fat (cream, ghee) and dietary fibre (3.33 g/100g) further attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (29 × 35.01) / 100 = 10.15, rounded to 10.2. Serving carbohydrate = 11.67 g/100g × 300 g / 100 = 35.01 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium, which is 35–40% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation; the high dietary fibre (3.33 g/100g) and potassium from lentils and tomatoes partially offset the sodium load, but cumulative sodium from accompaniments (rice, bread, pickles) must be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~29) from mixed lentils; high dietary fibre (3.33 g/100g) and protein (6.0 g/100g) slow glucose absorption; sugar content is very low at 1.0 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Dum Pukht is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200 g); the low-GI lentil base, high fibre, and protein content support glycemic control, but the caloric density from cream and ghee (150 kcal/100g) requires portion awareness to avoid excess energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100g from butter, cream, and ghee; a 300 g serving delivers 12 g saturated fat, approaching the 14–20 g daily limit for cardiovascular patients; trace trans fat is present at 0.17 g/100g from dairy sources.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced butter, cream, and ghee in preparation to lower saturated fat intake; the high dietary fibre from mixed lentils (3.33 g/100g) and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, garlic, ginger) are beneficial, but saturated fat and trans fat content warrant portion control and reduced-fat preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100g (below 5 g threshold); multiple anti-inflammatory ingredients including turmeric (curcumin), ginger (gingerols), garlic (allicin), and cumin/coriander (polyphenols).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal Dum Pukht is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and high-fibre lentil base; however, patients sensitive to nightshades (tomatoes, chilli) should monitor individual response, and reduced butter and cream preparation is advised to keep saturated fat well below 5 g/100g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Dum Pukht (also spelled Dal Dum Pukth) is a rich and creamy Indian mixed lentil dish prepared using the traditional dum pukht technique — a slow-cooking method in which ingredients are sealed in a heavy pot and cooked over low heat, allowing flavours to develop fully without moisture loss. The dish typically combines multiple lentil varieties including urad dal (black lentils), chana dal (split chickpeas), and toor dal (split pigeon peas), slow-simmered with tomatoes, onions, garlic, and ginger, then enriched with butter, cream, and ghee. A finishing tadka (tempering) of cumin, coriander, turmeric, and red chilli powder in ghee is added to intensify the aroma. The result is a velvety, deeply savoury curry with earthy lentil flavour, mild heat, and a luxurious creamy texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 150 kcal, 6.0 g protein, 8.33 g fat (4.0 g saturated), 11.67 g carbohydrate, 3.33 g dietary fibre, and 266.67 mg sodium. The glycemic index is low (~29), making it suitable for glycemic management. The high fibre and protein content support satiety and slow glucose absorption. Clinically, sodium content (266.67 mg/100g) requires monitoring for hypertensive patients, and saturated fat from dairy enrichment warrants portion control for cardiovascular patients. The dish is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a main course with basmati rice, naan, or roti in Indian restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Dal Dum Pukht as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked mixed lentil preparations and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for dairy enrichment components. The input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are plausible and consistent with ICMR-NIN reference ranges for enriched dal preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database lentil entries. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite restaurant-style dal preparation is not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; values would require weighted ingredient-level calculation beyond current source scope. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite preparation is not reported as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; estimated ~290 mg/100g from lentil and tomato components per ICMR-NIN ingredient data but not confirmed as a composite dish value, so -1 is retained. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dum pukht preparation are not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-17. trans_fat_g is 0.17 g/100g, consistent with trace dairy-derived trans fat from butter and ghee. All core macros (protein_g 6.0, fat_g 8.33, carbohydrate_g 11.67) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for an enriched mixed lentil curry. The original input source listed 'https://foodo.sg/' as the HPB Singapore URL — this URL is not the official HPB nutrient database and is a forbidden source; it has been replaced with ICMR-NIN (https://www.nin.res.in/nutrition_pub.html) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) as the primary approved nutrient sources. GI source is the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled lentils (GI 29) as an analogous estimate; estimation basis fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes.
